1: Nefesh (soul, disp 3) and ruach (Spirit, disp 4) are flagged -- Here is my servant...my chosen one, in whom I delight; I have placed my Spirit on him. The MKT renders ratseta nafshi as in whom I delight -- using soul as the idiom for the deepest personal pleasure (my soul delights = I fully delight). This is the idiom of deep personal satisfaction, not a clinical description. Matthew 3:16-17 and 12:18 quote this verse for Jesus' baptism and healing ministry -- the Spirit descending and the divine voice saying this is my Son in whom I am well pleased draws directly on this verse.
2: He will not shout or raise his voice, or make himself heard in the streets. The quietness of the Servant's ministry is the anti-triumphalist claim: the one on whom the Spirit rests does not announce himself through the normal channels of power and publicity. The MKT's make himself heard in the streets captures the Hebrew velo-yashmi in-hachutz qolo: will not cause his voice to be heard outside/in the street. The Servant's work is done without imperial self-promotion.
3: Emet (truth/faithfulness, disp 2) is flagged -- he will bring forth justice faithfully. The MKT renders emet as faithfully rather than truly -- capturing the reliability/steadiness dimension of emet rather than the propositional-truth dimension. The bruised reed not broken and the smoldering wick not snuffed: the Servant's tenderness toward the weak and nearly-extinguished. Matthew 12:20 quotes this verse for Jesus' non-coercive healing ministry.
4: He will not grow faint or be discouraged until he has established justice in the earth. The perseverance of the Servant mirrors the gentleness of v3: the same one who will not break a bruised reed will not quit until the whole earth has been reached with justice. The coastlands will wait for his instruction -- the global scope of the Servant's mission (all the way to the coastlands) is established before the specific commissioning of vv5-7.
5: YHWH and ruach (spirit, disp 4) are flagged -- God the LORD (ha-El YHWH) who created the heavens, stretched them out, spread out the earth, who gives neshamah (breath) to the people on it and ruach (spirit) to those who walk on it. The MKT distinguishes neshamah (breath, the animating breath of life, Genesis 2:7) from ruach (the vitalizing spirit that animates walking creatures). Both together describe the comprehensive life-giving of the Creator God who is speaking to the Servant.
6: YHWH and berit (covenant, disp 3) are flagged -- I, the LORD, have called you in righteousness...I will make you a covenant for the people. The Servant as berit-am (covenant of/for the people) -- the Servant embodies the covenant rather than merely receiving it. The MKT's a covenant for the people is the correct translation: the Servant is the instrument through which the covenant relationship is established with the people. The pairing with a light for the nations expands the scope to universal. Hebrews 8:6-13 develops the new covenant the Servant mediates.
7: The Servant's mission: open blind eyes, bring prisoners from the dungeon, release those in darkness. The three acts of liberation -- sight, freedom, and light -- become the vocabulary of Jesus' mission statement (Luke 4:18-19 citing Isaiah 61:1-2, but the same liberatory cluster). The MKT's dungeon and prison are parallel -- different words for the same confinement. The Servant's mission is physical and spiritual liberation.
8: YHWH and kavod (glory, disp 2) are flagged -- I am the LORD; that is my name; I will not give my glory to another nor my praise to carved idols. The divine-name assertion and the refusal to share glory is one of the most theologically dense statements in Isaiah. The divine name YHWH is what makes this refusal of shared glory non-arbitrary: the covenant name defines a unique divine identity that cannot be distributed to other claimants. The glory (kavod) is the weight/honor that belongs to the one true God. The NT applies divine glory to Christ (John 17:5) without contradiction, since Christ shares the divine name.
9: The former things have come to pass -- YHWH's track record of fulfilled prediction. New things are now declared, announced before they spring up. The pattern of prediction-then-fulfillment is YHWH's way of demonstrating divine reliability and distinction from idols (who could predict nothing, Isaiah 41). The MKT's before they spring up I announce them captures the temporal precedence of divine announcement over historical event.
10: YHWH is flagged -- Sing to the LORD a new song. The new song (shir chadash) called for by the new things: as YHWH does a new thing, the appropriate response is a new song, not the recycling of old thanksgiving forms. The scope of the singing -- from sea-travelers to desert-towns (Kedar) to mountain-tops (Sela) -- is universal. The MKT's ends of the earth matches the scope of the Servant's mission (v4: the coastlands).
11: Kedar (Arabian) and Sela (Edomite) raise their voices -- the non-Israelite peoples of the region joining the new song. The geographical specificity is important: these are not abstractions but named peoples with their own histories and territories, invited into the universal praise. The MKT's villages where Kedar dwells and inhabitants of Sela preserve the historical particularity.
12: YHWH and kavod (glory, disp 2) are flagged -- let them ascribe glory to the LORD and proclaim his praise in the coastlands. The ascription of glory to the LORD rather than to Cyrus (the human instrument) or to the nations themselves is the corrective to the imperial tendency to self-glorify. The coastlands who were terrified (Isaiah 41:5) are now called to praise -- fear transformed into worship.
13: YHWH is flagged -- The LORD marches out like a warrior, like a man of war he arouses his zeal. The divine warrior image: YHWH as a warrior who cries out, shouts, and triumphs over his enemies. This is the same image as Exodus 15:3 (the LORD is a warrior) developed in a new context. The zeal (qinah: jealous burning passion) aroused in YHWH is the covenantal-love dimension of divine warfare: he fights for his people because he loves them jealously.
14: Olam (ancient time, disp 3) is flagged -- for a long time I have kept silent; I have been still and held myself back. The MKT renders me-olam as for a long time -- the age-long duration rather than absolute eternity. The period of divine silence during exile (olam) is the duration of the people's suffering. Now I will cry out like a woman in labor -- the breaking of divine silence with the urgency and pain of childbirth. The woman-in-labor image for YHWH's speaking is remarkable and maternal.
15: I will lay waste mountains and hills, dry up vegetation, turn rivers to dry land -- the scale of divine action is creation-wide. But this destructive action is in service of the restoration of vv16-17: the clearing away of obstacles to enable the blind to be led. The environmental disruption of v15 creates the leveled path of v16. The MKT preserves the active divine verbs (I will lay waste, I will dry up, I will turn) throughout.
16: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged twice -- I will lead the blind along a road they have not known; I will guide them on paths they have never walked. I will turn the darkness into light before them and the rough ground into a smooth place. The double yada (never known / never walked) emphasizes total unfamiliarity -- the road is completely new, not a familiar path with better lighting. The MKT's never known and never walked are both from the same root, showing the consistency of the Hebrew's emphasis on newness.
17: Elohim is flagged -- those who trust in carved idols and say to cast images, You are our gods. The word Elohim in the mouths of the idol-worshippers: they call their images elohim, the plural word for God. The divine name itself is transferred to false objects. Those who do this will be turned back and completely put to shame (bosh yevoshu: double shame-verb) -- the intensity of the shame matches the intensity of the misidentification.
18: The summons to the deaf and blind to hear and see. The deaf and blind of v18 are specifically Israel (as v19 clarifies) -- not the nations' idolaters but the covenant people who have eyes but don't see, ears but don't hear. The command to the blind to look and see is the same paradox as Ezekiel 12:2: a people who have the capacity for perception but have not used it. The MKT's imperative forms (Hear! Look! See!) match the Hebrew's urgency.
19: YHWH is flagged -- the paradox: Who is blind but my servant, or deaf like my messenger whom I send? Israel is YHWH's servant and messenger -- but the servant is blind, the messenger is deaf. This is the sharpest critique in the chapter: the one commissioned to carry divine sight and hearing to others cannot see or hear. The MKT's my dedicated one (meshullam: the one at peace, completed) maintains the irony of the sealed/complete one being blind.
20: He sees many things but does not take them in; his ears are open but he does not hear. The distinction between seeing and perceiving, hearing and registering -- the physical faculties are present but the responsive capacity is closed. This failure of perception is what the Servant of Isaiah 42:1-9 will cure in others (vv6-7) but which describes the corporate servant Israel's own condition. The MKT's does not take them in is a good rendering of lo yishmor: does not keep/guard what is seen.
21: YHWH is flagged -- it pleased the LORD, for the sake of his righteousness, to make his law great and glorious. The magnification of the Torah is YHWH's own pleasure and his righteous purpose. The glorious (addir: magnificent, majestic) Torah is the object of the LORD's pleasure. This contrasts with the people's failure to hear (v20): the Torah is magnificent, but the servant Israel has not listened to it. The law is not the problem; the hearing is.
22: The consequence of not hearing: Israel robbed and plundered, trapped in holes, hidden in dungeons, taken as prey, with no rescuer. The prison imagery reverses v7 (bringing prisoners out of dungeons) -- Israel is now in the very dungeon the Servant would release them from. The ones who should carry liberation are themselves imprisoned. The MKT's trapped in holes and hidden in dungeons preserves the double imprisonment-imagery.
23: The rhetorical summons to attention: Who among you will pay attention to this? Who will listen and hear for what is to come? The call to hear (v18) now becomes a question about whether anyone will. The hearing for what is to come means hearing with prospective orientation -- not just registering past events but being formed by divine word to face the future. The MKT's pay attention renders yashev: who will sit/give ear to this?
24: YHWH is flagged -- Who handed Jacob over to plunderers...Was it not the LORD, against whom we have sinned? The theodicy question answered: the LORD handed Israel over. But the reason given is not arbitrary -- it was because they sinned, refused to walk in his ways, and did not obey his law. The acknowledgment of divine agency in the exile (the LORD handed over) is paired with the acknowledgment of Israel's responsibility (against whom we have sinned). Both are necessary for the repentance the text calls for.
25: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- it set him ablaze all around, but he did not understand; it burned against him, but he did not take it to heart. The yada here is rendered understand: Israel did not know/understand the divine message encoded in the suffering of the burning anger and the battle poured out. The failure to learn from judgment is the same failure as the failure to learn from blessing (vv18-20): both forms of divine communication are unheard.
